publicationstmt.publisher: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

controlnote.p: This finding aid, which is compliant with the Yale EAD Best Practice Guidelines, Version 1.0., has a MARC record in Yale's ILS with the following bib number: 3765431.

maintenanceagency.agencyname: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

descriptivenote.p: Finding aid description and metadata in Archives at Yale are licensed under a CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. This means that anyone may copy, modify, and distribute this descriptive metadata without restriction or permission. The CC0 license is explained in full on the Creative Commons website: CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. More information is available on our Help page.

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Wednesday January 20, 2021 at 04:14

corpname.part: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

did.abstract: The Norman Holmes Pearson papers consist of correspondence, writings, printed material, photographs, and audiovisual material that document a broad range of Pearson's professional and personal activities, including his research on Nathaniel Hawthorn and Ezra Pound, his management of the literary estates of H.D. and Bryher, and his activity as a professor of English and American Studies at Yale University. Documentation relating to Pearson's service in the Office of Strategic Services is partially redacted.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into eleven series: I. Correspondence, 1839-1976. II. Editorial and Other Projects, circa 1880s-1975. III. Writings, 1931-1968. IV. Research Files, 1851-1978. V. Professional Papers, 1932-1979. VI. Personal Papers, 1888-1975. VII. Photographs and Microfilm, circa 1920-1941. VIII. Printed Material, circa 1890-1975. IX. Audiovisual Material, 1963-1985. X. Realia, undated. XI. Material Removed From Books, circa 1960-1975.

bioghist.head: Norman Holmes Pearson (1909-1975)
bioghist.p: Norman Holmes Pearson (Yale 1932), professor of English and American Studies at Yale University and officer in the Office of Strategic Services during the Second World War. Pearson collaborated with curator Donald Gallup to develop the Yale Collection of American Literature, playing an instrumental role in the acquisition of the papers of H.D. and Bryher.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: In-process collection. In January 2019 this collection closed temporarily to readers. As of 28 September, 2020, Series I. Correspondence is available. Portions of the collection may be made available when processing is complete. For more information, please consult the Closed Collections Schedule on the Beinecke Web site at https://beinecke.library.yale.edu/collections/closed-collections-schedule.
Box 228 (audiovisual material): Restricted fragile material. Reference copies may be requested. Consult Access Services for further information.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The Norman Holmes Pearson papers consist of correspondence, writings, printed material, photographs, and audiovisual material that document a broad range of Pearson's professional and personal activities, including his research on Nathaniel Hawthorne and Ezra Pound, his management of the literary estates of H.D. and Bryher, and his activity as a professor of English and American Studies at Yale University. Documentation relating to Pearson's service in the Office of Strategic Services is partially redacted.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Former call numbers: Za Pearson and Uncat Za Pearson.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Norman Holmes Pearson Papers. Yale Collection of American Literature, Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Gift and bequest of Norman Holmes Pearson, 1966-1975.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: The Norman Holmes Pearson Papers is the physical property of the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University. Literary rights, including copyright, belong to the authors or their legal heirs and assigns. For further information, consult the appropriate curator.

corpname.part: United States. Office of Strategic Services

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into three subseries: General Correspondence, Family Correspondence, and Third Party Correspondence. General Correspondence is arranged alphabetically by correspondent or heading and chronologically within files. Family Correspondence is subdivided for letters to and from Norman Holmes Pearson and third party letters. Third Party Correspondence is arranged alphabetically.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series I. Correspondence consists of personal, professional, family, and third party correspondence. Materials document Pearson's work as a university professor, including his role in the American Studies community in the United States and abroad, and development of Yale's literary manuscript holdings.

General Correpondence is largely professional in nature and includes both named files and subject-, project-, and event-oriented files. The later, such as "American Studies," "Fulbright Scholarship," and "Doshisha seminar" have been preserved as found among Pearson's files or earlier processing of the collection. Folder headings are transcribed and in quotation marks.

Corporate files can be found for college and university offices, departments, libraries, and presses, commercial publishers, booksellers, journals, and literary and cultural organizations. Correspondence with college and university offices often relates to lecture invitations. Correspondence with faculty of different departments often relates either to departmental searches for candidates or personal requests for recommendations, though sometimes concerns projects or books. Correspondence with libraries relates to research.

Named files for correspondence with individuals include writers, colleagues in literary and American studies, and former students, among others. Along with early curator of the Yale Collection of American Literature, Donald Gallup, Pearson played an important role in developing Yale's literary manuscript holdings; he had a hand, for example, in the acquisitions of the papers of H.D., Bryher, and Eugene O'Neill. He knew H.D., Bryher, and Ezra Poud, and communicated with others in their family and social circles, including H.D.'s daughter Perdita Schaffner, Richard Aldington, Islay, Kenneth Macpherson, and Robert McAlmon, as well as other domestic Imagists, including John Gould Fletcher. In some cases Pearson played a role in finding publishers for their work. He also served as executor for H.D. and Bryher.
Large files in the General Correspondence subseries include: Bryher, Horace Gregory, Sherri Martinelli, Ezra Pound, and William Carlos Williams.

Carbons of outgoing typescript letters from Pearson are common in this subseries. Pearson also sometimes wrote on the recto of letters to him, typically re-used academic documents or correspondence. One can also find manuscript notes in Pearson's hand in the correspondence folders.
Enclosures, such as third-party letters, writings, photographs, and financial records, can be found accompanying letters and are often described in folder notes. In cases where other materials were prevalent in correspondence files but could not be established as enclosures, such materials have been removed to a separate folder(s). For example, following several folders of correspondence with Robert Duncan, there is a folder for "Printed materials removed from Duncan, Robert." Folders of third party correspondence relating to a particular individual have also been preserved.
General correspondence collection materials chiefly in English, with some French, German, and Italian, and enclosures in Japanese and Hungarian.

In addition to the large run of General Correspondence documenting Pearson's personal and professional activities, there are letters to and from Pearson with members of the Bennett, Kittredge, Pearson, and other families. Of particular note is the decades-long and rich exchange between Pearson and his mother, Fanny H. (Kittredge) Pearson. Approximately 1,000 pieces of correspondence date from 1920 to 1963.

There is also a small quantity of third party correspondence consisting of contemporary material that likely came into Pearson's possession as enclosures, subsequently separated from accompanying correspondence, and older correspondence possibly acquired by Pearson for research or for the library.
Note that additional correspondence can be found among the mixed files for editorial and publishing projects in Series II. Editorial and Other Projects.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Arranged alphabetically by correspondent or heading.

did.unittitle: Bread Loaf School of English (Middlebury College)
Bryan, C. D. B. (Courtlandt Dixon Barnes)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Full run of Bryher correspondence (147 folders) includes enclosures, such as drafts of writings and clippings; correspondence from Bryher with notes from H.D., Kenneth Macpherson, Islay Lyons, and others; third-party correspondence to Bryher, including one autograph postcard, signed, from Carl Van Vechten and one typescript letter, signed, from Horace Gregory; two autograph letters, signed, from D. H. Lawrence to Dorothy Richardson; and correspondence from Bryher addressed to Susan Pearson

scopecontent.p: Includes photographs of Bryher, H.D., and Pearson
